coredata.dc:title: Construction of 0D/2D heterojunction in 3D MXene/GQDs hybrid aerogels for enhanced broad electromagnetic wave absorption

coredata.dc:description: Significant efforts have been made towards designing and fabricating MXene/carbon hybrid aerogels for electromagnetic wave (EMW) absorption applications. Carbon nano-species, such as 2D graphene and 1D carbon nanotubes (CNTs), have been widely incorporated into MXene/carbon aerogels. However, they often exhibit poor impedance matching due to their high conductivity, thus limiting further improvements in EMW absorption performance. Unlike 2D graphene and 1D CNTs/nanofibers, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) exhibit intriguing chemical and physical properties, as well as a zero-band electronic structure. To the best of our knowledge, the EMW response behavior of 0D/2D MXene/GQDs heterojunction in 3D aerogels has not yet been explored. Herein, we develop a unique approach for the controlled synthesis of 3D MXene/GQDs composite aerogels featuring with abundant 0D/2D heterojunctions. It was found that GQDs have a significant effect on the microstructure of MXene/GQDs aerogels. As the amount of GQD increases, more developed porous structures in aerogels are formed. The introduction of GQDs leads to the formation of numerous 0D/2D heterointerfaces and plays a key role in modulating the electromagnetic (EM) parameters and optimizing the impedance matching of 3D MXene/GQDs aerogels, thereby enhancing their absorption capabilities. The obtained MXene/GQDs aerogels exhibit a remarkable EMW response behavior, with a strong minimum reflection loss (RLmin) of −53.21 dB and a broad effective absorption bandwidth (EAB) of 7.90 GHz at 2.5 mm. The multiple dissipation modes and optimized impedance matching significantly contribute to the enhanced EMW absorption capabilities. This investigation offers a new boulevard for developing advanced 3D MXene-based hybrid aerogels beyond EM response.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Illustration of the fabrication process of MXene/GQDs aerogels. (b, c) TEM, (d) HRTEM, (e) EDS mapping of MXene/GQDs aerogel (M/G-8/1). (f) SEM images of MXene/GQDs aerogels with various GQDs contents: pure MXene (ⅰ), M/G-8/0.5 (ⅱ), M/G-8/1 (ⅲ), M/G-8/2 (ⅳ).

(a) XRD patterns, (b) Raman spectroscopy of pure MXene and MXene/GQDs aerogels with various GQDs contents. (c) Full survey spectra, (d) Cumulative pore volume and high-resolution XPS spectra of C (e), Ti (f), O (g), and F (h) elements of pure MXene and MXene/GQDs aerogel (M/G-8/1).

EMW absorption performance of MXene/GQDs aerogels. 2D RL contours (a) and RL curves (b) for MXene/GQDs aerogels with various GQDs contents: pure MXene (ⅰ), M/G-8/0.5 (ⅱ), M/G-8/1 (ⅲ), M/G-8/2 (ⅳ). (c, d) Histogram of MXene/GQDs aerogels with EAB and RLmin values. (e) Comparison of MXene/based aerogels.

Real part of permittivity constant (a), imaginary part of permittivity constant (b), dielectric loss tanδ e (c), attenuation constant α (d), average real part of permittivity constant (e) and average imaginary part of permittivity constant (f) of MXene/GQDs aerogels. Cole-Cole plots(g): pure MXene (ⅰ) and M/G-8/1 (ⅱ). The impedance value maps (h): pure MXene (ⅰ), M/G-8/0.5 (ⅱ), M/G-8/1 (ⅲ), M/G-8/2 (ⅳ).

Schematic diagram of EMW absorption mechanism of MXene/GQDs hybrid aerogels: (a) EMW transmission mechanism, (b) multiple scattering mechanism, (c) interfacial polarization mechanism, (d) dipole polarization mechanism and (e) conductive loss mechanism.

head.ce:title: Construction of 0D/2D heterojunction in 3D MXene/GQDs hybrid aerogels for enhanced broad electromagnetic wave absorption

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Significant efforts have been made towards designing and fabricating MXene/carbon hybrid aerogels for electromagnetic wave (EMW) absorption applications. Carbon nano-species, such as 2D graphene and 1D carbon nanotubes (CNTs), have been widely incorporated into MXene/carbon aerogels. However, they often exhibit poor impedance matching due to their high conductivity, thus limiting further improvements in EMW absorption performance. Unlike 2D graphene and 1D CNTs/nanofibers, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) exhibit intriguing chemical and physical properties, as well as a zero-band electronic structure. To the best of our knowledge, the EMW response behavior of 0D/2D MXene/GQDs heterojunction in 3D aerogels has not yet been explored. Herein, we develop a unique approach for the controlled synthesis of 3D MXene/GQDs composite aerogels featuring with abundant 0D/2D heterojunctions. It was found that GQDs have a significant effect on the microstructure of MXene/GQDs aerogels. As the amount of GQD increases, more developed porous structures in aerogels are formed. The introduction of GQDs leads to the formation of numerous 0D/2D heterointerfaces and plays a key role in modulating the electromagnetic (EM) parameters and optimizing the impedance matching of 3D MXene/GQDs aerogels, thereby enhancing their absorption capabilities. The obtained MXene/GQDs aerogels exhibit a remarkable EMW response behavior, with a strong minimum reflection loss (RLmin) of −53.21 dB and a broad effective absorption bandwidth (EAB) of 7.90 GHz at 2.5 mm. The multiple dissipation modes and optimized impedance matching significantly contribute to the enhanced EMW absorption capabilities. This investigation offers a new boulevard for developing advanced 3D MXene-based hybrid aerogels beyond EM response.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid advancement of radio communication technology, electromagnetic pollution has escalated significantly, which not only disrupts the normal operation of sophisticated electronic devices but also poses radiation hazards to human health. Electromagnetic wave (EMW) absorbing materials are capable of absorbing and attenuating EMW and have arisen as the most capable candidates to resolve EM radiation challenges in both military and civilian sectors[1,2]. However, it remains a challenging task to explore high-efficiency EMW absorbing materials that feature properties of “strong, broad, thin and lightweight”. Two-dimensional (2D) materials (such as graphene and MoS2) have recently garnered significant interest in EMW response applications owing to their exclusive lamellar structure, peculiar electrical properties, large specific surface area, and excellent mechanical strength[3,4]. In particular, 2D transition metal carbides/nitrides (MXene) have exhibited strong and broadband EMW dissipation ability as a result of surface defects, inherent high conductivity, and abundant surface terminal groups[5–7]. Moreover, the versatile surface terminal groups endow MXene sheets with solution-processability and superior chemical affinity to other materials, thereby enabling the modulation of electromagnetic parameters to effectively interact with incident EMW. However, analogous to other 2D materials, MXene nanosheets tend to face-to-face self-restacking and random agglomeration due to their inherent van der Waals forces, leading to large density and decreased surface polarization loss [8–10].

Integrating 2D nanosheets into a 3D porous architecture favors to alleviate the tendency of self-restacking and diminishes density. Notably, high porosity, large specific surface area, and ultralight characteristics endow aerogels with significant advantages for EMW absorption. The porous architecture of aerogels is conducive to impedance matching, allowing more incident EMW to enter absorbers and thereby enhancing EMW absorption performance[11,12]. Moreover, the high porosity and large specific surface area of aerogels introduce abundant interfaces and tortuous spaces that confine EMW within the absorbers through multiple reflections and scattering, which enhances the interaction between the incident EMW and the absorbers. Additionally, the ultralow density of aerogels makes them highly competitive candidates for future lightweight, high-performance absorbers. The emergence of MXene has driven the rapid development of advanced MXene aerogels. Since single-component absorbers struggle to simultaneously achieve strong attenuation capacity and impedance matching, multi-component hybridizations have increasingly become the focus of research on high-efficiency EMW absorbing materials. The synergistic effects of different components can enhance loss mechanisms and optimize impedance matching. Consequently, significant efforts have been made towards designing and fabricating MXene-based hybrid aerogels[13–15]. Carbon nano-species, such as 2D graphene and 1D carbon nanotubes (CNTs), have been widely incorporated into MXene/carbon hybrid aerogels due to their good conductivity and lightweight nature[16]. For instance, Li et al. assembled a hollow core–shell reduced graphene oxide (RGO)/Ti3C2TX hybrid foam using a sacrificial template strategy, achieving a RLmin of −20 dB along with a broad EAB covering the full X-band at 3.2 mm[17]. Similarly, Cui et al. constructed an MXene/CNTs/PI aerogel with an enhanced EAB of 5.6 GHz[18]. In these MXene/carbon hybrid aerogels, the newly created heterogeneous interface between MXene and carbon materials gives rise to interfacial polarization, thereby enhancing EMW dissipation[19–21]. However, the dense electron transmission network of MXene/carbon hybrid aerogels, as a result of the high conductivity and large aspect ratio of the two components, causes weak impedance matching, thereby limiting further improvement in EMW absorption capacity. Unlike 2D graphene and 1D carbon nanotubes/nanofibers, 0D graphene quantum dots (GQDs) exhibit intriguing chemical and physical properties and a zero-band electronic structure due to their single atomic layer structure and quantum confinement effect[22,23]. To the best of our knowledge, the EMW response behavior of 0D/2D MXene/GQDs aerogels has not been explored so far. In this regard, it is indispensable to construct a 0D/2D heterojunction of MXene/GQDs aerogel, which may combine the respective structural virtues of the components and thus realize outstanding EMW absorption performance.

Herein, we demonstrated the synthesis of MXene/GQDs composite aerogel by integrating MXene nanosheets with GQDs using an ice-templated freeze-drying method. It was found that GQDs are uniformly grafted onto the surface of Ti3C2TX MXene flakes and have a significant impact on the microstructure of MXene/GQDs aerogels. The introduction of GQDs leads to the formation of numerous heterointerfaces and plays an important role in modulating the EM parameters and optimizing the impedance matching of MXene/GQDs aerogels, thus enhancing their absorption capability. The obtained MXene/GQDs aerogels exhibit an attractive EMW response, accomplishing a RLmin of −53.21 dB along with a broad EAB of 7.90 GHz at 2.5 mm. The multiple dissipation modes and optimized impedance matching significantly contribute to the enhanced EMW absorption capabilities. This work opens a novel pathway for developing high-efficiency EMW absorbing materials.

Ti3C2TX MXene was prepared via etching and delaminating MAX phase Ti3AlC2 [24]. First, 2 g of LiF was added into 40 mL of 9 M HCl in a PTFE cup with stirring for 10 minutes. Then, the acid solution containing 2 g of Ti3AlC2 powder was prepared and the mixture was suffered from 35 °C with long time of 24 h for the corrosion of the Al layers, yielding a multilayer MXene solution. Subsequently, the solution was washed and centrifuged (5000 rpm, 10 minutes) until the pH was up to 6. After that, we collected deposit and sonicated for 1 hour to further expand the layer intervals. Following centrifugation at 10000 rpm, the Ti3C2TX MXene solid was redispersed in water, and the supernatant was collected as a single-layer Ti3C2TX MXene suspension.

The GQDs (graphene quantum dots) used in this work were synthesized using the photo-Fenton reaction of graphene oxide, according to our previous studies. Initially, different amounts of 1 mg/mL GQDs aqueous solution were blended with 8 mL of 8 mg/mL Ti3C2TX MXene under room-temperature stirring. The mixture was then frozen and lyophilized to obtain MXene/GQDs hybrid aerogels. The obtained MXene/GQDs hybrid aerogels were designated as M/G-8/2, M/G-8/1, and M/G-8/0.5, according to the volumes of GQDs added: 2、1 and 0.5 mL, respectively. The pure MXene aerogel without GQDs was designated as MXene-8.

The morphologies analysis was obtained by TEM (JEM-2010F T, JEOL, Japan) on an operated at 200 kV and an Apreo field-emission SEM (FEI, USA). AFM image was performed on a Multimode 8 scanning probe microscopy system (Bruker, USA) by tapping mode. The chemical state was collected via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum on K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) using a monochromated Al Kα source at 15 kV, as well as Raman spectra (Renishaw, UK) with 514 nm excitation wavelength of laser. The crystal structure was recorded by X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker, USA) with a Cu-Kα radiation source at the scan rate of 5° min−1. The Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore-size distributions were acquired using Autosorb-iQ instrument (Quantachrome, USA).

The molten paraffin wax was first used to impregnate MXene/GQDs aerogel monoliths. After the paraffin solidified, the aerogels were further processed into the desired circular ring with 3.04 mm for inner diameter (Φin) and 7.00 mm for outer diameter (Φout). Using a coaxial test system with an Agilent N5230 vector network analyzer, EM parameters were obtained. Reflection loss (RL) values indicating the absorption capacity of the absorbers were calculated based on the electromagnetic parameters as follows[25].

The construction procedure of the MXene/GQDs (graphene quantum dots) composite aerogels is illustrated in Fig. 1a and involves integrating Ti3C2TX MXene nanosheets with GQDs in an aqueous solution, followed by ice-templated freeze-drying. The GQDs aqueous solution was synthesized using the photo-assisted hydrogen peroxide oxidation, specifically the Photo-Fenton reaction of graphene oxide. Meanwhile, selectively etching Al layers from MAX phase Ti3AlC2 powder using LiF/HCl was employed for synthesizing Ti3C2TX MXene, followed by exfoliating the multilayer accordion-like product via ultrasonic treatment. The morphology and structural characterization confirm that the obtained Ti3C2TX MXene is monolayer. SEM images (Figure S1b) clearly reveal the two-dimensional planar structure of Ti3C2TX MXene with lateral sizes of several microns, while TEM images (Figure S1c) show that the Ti3C2TX MXene sheets are transparent and ultrathin, demonstrating the successful etching and ultrasonic exfoliation of the granular Ti3AlC2 precursor [27]. The SAED pattern (Figure S1d) confirms its hexagonal atomic packing crystalline structure [28]. The surface and edges of Ti3C2TX MXene sheets exhibit numerous small particles and tiny holes resulting from oxidation in the fabrication process. Copious terminal groups (-O, -OH, and -F) are introduced onto Ti3C2TX MXene flakes, imparting solution-processability and superior chemical affinity for surface modification or the formation of heterogeneous structures, thereby modulating the electromagnetic parameters to effectively respond to EMW [29]. Meanwhile, the TEM images confirmed the presence of point defects and line defects in MXene (Figure S2, Supporting Information). The surface terminal groups (-O, -OH, and -F) and structure defects are responsible for dipole polarization and defect polarization to dissipate EMW. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) further confirms the two-dimensional planar structure of Ti3C2TX MXene flakes and reveals a thickness of 1.5 nm measured from the topographic height profile in the AFM image (Figure S3, Supporting Information) [30]. The morphology of GQDs used in this work is also characterized by AFM, which shows that the as-generated GQDs have a uniform size distribution around several nanometers and a thickness of 1.3 nm measured from the topographic height profile (Figure S4, Supporting Information) [31]. The oxidation reaction during the fabrication process introduces numerous oxygen-containing functional groups (-O, -OH, and -COOH) onto the carbon skeleton of GQDs [32]. The planar structure and oxygen-containing functional groups allow for strong interactions between GQDs and MXene sheets via van der Waals forces and hydrogen bonding, thus facilitating the formation of a homogeneous MXene/GQDs hybrid. As displayed in Fig. 1b and c, GQDs are densely and uniformly decorated on the surface of Ti3C2TX MXene flakes without noticeable agglomeration, with an average size of 2.7 nm. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM, Fig. 1d) and its fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern (inset in Fig. 1d) exhibit distinct lattice spacing of 0.214 nm, corresponding to the (100) facet of graphene [33,34]. The EDS mappings (Fig. 1e) demonstrate the uniform distribution of F and O elements in the MXene/GQDs composite, which originates from the surface terminal groups on both Ti3C2TX MXene flakes and the carbon skeleton of GQDs.

SEM was conducted to reveal the microstructure of MXene/GQDs aerogels with various GQDs contents, as shown in Fig. 1f. All the MXene/GQDs aerogel samples display chaotic 3D porous networks, which is generated by wrinkled, thick, and overlapped layers with lateral sizes of several hundred microns, assembled from single-layered Ti3C2TX MXene sheets. It was observed that the amount of GQDs significantly affects the microstructure of the MXene/GQDs aerogels. With increasing GQDs addition, MXene layers exhibit smaller lateral sizes and a more wrinkled surface, indicating that GQDs can impede the stacking of MXene nanosheets. Given the same initial content of Ti3C2TX MXene sheets before assembly, the smaller lateral sizes tend to form more developed porous structures in the MXene/GQDs aerogel monoliths. This more developed porous structure contributes significantly to providing abundant interfaces and tortuous spaces that confine electromagnetic waves (EMW) in the absorbers through multiple reflections and scattering, thereby strengthening the interaction between incident EMW and the absorbers to promote EMW consumption [35]. In addition, as shown in Fig. 2d, compared to pure MXene aerogel, the MXene/GQDs aerogel has a wider pore distribution. The porous network structure renders MXene/GQDs aerogels ultralow density, allowing them to rest on a leaf. A digital image of the MXene/GQDs aerogel is provided in Fig. 1a.

The structural information of MXene/GQDs hybrid aerogels was further investigated using XRD patterns and Raman spectroscopy. The XRD pattern of the precursor Ti3AlC2 powder (Figure S5, Supporting Information) displays multiple sharp diffraction peaks, while only a single peak at 2θ≈ 7.59°, assigned to the (002) peak, is observed in Ti3C2TX MXene (Fig. 2a) [36]. This indicates the Al layers have been completely removed and no impurity exists in Ti3C2TX MXene. For MXene/GQDs aerogels, with increasing GQDs content, the leftward shift of the (002) peak to lower angles indicates an expanded interlayer spacing, suggesting that the restacking of MXene sheets is better suppressed due to the intercalation of GQDs between the MXene nanosheets [37,38]. The Raman spectrum of pure Ti3C2TX MXene and MXene/GQDs aerogels is shown in Fig. 2b. Peaks at 159, 197, 395, and 612 cm⁻¹ correspond to the Ti3C2TX phase [39], while two peaks at 1413 and 1565 cm⁻¹ are associated with the D and G bands of carbon. The G band originates from the vibration of sp² carbon atoms, and the D band corresponds to the vibration of sp³ defects [40]. The increasing ratio of the D to G bands from MXene to M/G-8/2 is attributed to the introduced carbon defects by GQDs. The chemical state of MXene/GQDs aerogels was investigated using XPS spectroscopy. The full survey spectra are displayed in Fig. 2c, indicating the existence of Ti, C, O, and F in both pure Ti3C2TX MXene and MXene/GQDs aerogel (M/G-8/1). Fig. 2e-h present the high-resolution XPS spectra of C, Ti, O, and F elements, respectively. The C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2e) is decomposed into components corresponding to C-Ti (282.0 eV), C-C (284.8 eV), C-O (287.0 eV), and C-F (288.5 eV), indicating that there is good preservation of Ti3C2 structures after HF etching and that terminal groups (-O, -OH, and -F) are present on the carbon species. The increased C-O peak intensity observed in the MXene/GQDs aerogel is attributed to the attendance of oxygen-containing functional groups in the GQDs. The deconvoluted O 1 s spectrum further reveals surface terminations, specifically Ti-O (529.4 eV), C-Ti-Ox (531.4 eV), C-Ti-OH (532.3 eV), and absorbed H₂O (533.8 eV) (Fig. 2g) [41]. The reduced C-Ti-OH peak intensity in the MXene/GQDs aerogel may be attributed to the fact that hydrogen bonds emerge between the terminal groups of Ti3C2TX MXene flakes and GQDs.

The microstructure and spatial configuration of aerogels are crucial in determining their electromagnetic wave (EMW) absorption performance [42]. To preserve these characteristics, MXene/GQDs aerogel monoliths were impregnated with molten paraffin wax, deviating from the conventional blending method, to prepare samples for recording EM parameters. The 2D Reflection loss (RL) contours and RL curves for MXene/GQDs aerogels with varying GQDs content are shown in Fig. 3a and b. The pure MXene aerogel, with no added GQDs, exhibits weak RLmin of −12.33 dB and a narrow EAB of 3.36 GHz. In contrast, MXene/GQDs hybrid aerogels demonstrate improved RL values and EAB. Specifically, M/G-8/0.5 significantly enhances EMW absorption, showing a RLmin of −19.94 dB and an EAB of 5.28 GHz (12.72–18.0 GHz) at 2.1 mm. Notably, M/G-8/1 further optimizes RLmin to −53.21 dB at 12.88 GHz and achieves a widest EAB of 7.90 GHz at 2.5 mm, surpassing most MXene-based aerogels (Fig. 3e). Additionally, adjusting the thickness between 2.0 and 4.0 mm can make the effective absorption of M/G-8/1 span the entire Ku/X bands with part of the C band. However, excessive GQDs addition detracts from the absorption properties of MXene/GQDs aerogels. For instance, M/G-8/2, with a higher GQDs content, shows a RLmin of −50.94 dB and an EAB of 7.20 GHz at 2.4 mm. This indicates that while GQDs significantly enhance EMW absorption performance, excessive amounts can be detrimental. Additionally, the initial concentration of Ti3C2TX MXene dispersion also affects EMW absorption performance. The 2D RL contours of MXene/GQDs aerogels with a higher initial Ti3C2TX concentration (12 mg/mL) are provided in Figure S6 (Supporting Information). For M/G-12/0.5, a RLmin of −28.91 dB and EAB of 5.20 GHz at 1.8 mm are achieved. In comparison, M/G-12/1 shows a RLmin of −38.38 dB and an EAB of 4.96 GHz at 2.8 mm. Aerogels with a higher starting concentration of Ti3C2TX MXene exhibit reduced EMW absorption performance, likely due to their denser 3D porous network structure.

Reflection loss (RL) is closely related to EM parameters including complex permittivity/permeability, which are crucial in determining EMW absorption performance of materials [43–45]. Given the non-magnetic nature of both Ti3C2TX MXene and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), this study focuses solely on complex permittivity associated to dielectric loss to elucidate the EMW response behavior of MXene/GQDs aerogels. Fig. 4a and b presents complex permittivity for MXene/GQDs aerogels with varying GQD content. The real parts (ε') values generally decrease as the EMW frequency rises from 2 to 18 GHz. This reduction in ε' at higher frequencies can be attributed to frequency dispersion, where electric dipoles exhibit a delayed response to rapidly fluctuating external electromagnetic fields [46]. Notably, the ε' values decline with increasing GQD content, as illustrated by the average ε' values dropping from 10.08 to 5.58 in Fig. 4e. Similarly, imaginary parts (ε") exhibits a comparable trend (Fig. 4f). According to free electron theory, ε" values correlate positively with material electrical conductivity. Given that GQDs, similar to graphene oxide, contain numerous oxygen-containing functional groups on their carbon skeleton, they exhibit relatively poor electrical conductivity. Consequently, the incorporation of GQDs increases the barrier to electronic transmission, leading to reduced electrical conductivity in MXene/GQDs aerogels. The slightly elevated ε" value observed for M/G-8/2 may be due to the more extensive porous network within the insulating matrix, which provides enhanced conductive pathways for electron hopping and migration. Additionally, ε' and ε" values of MXene/GQDs hybrid aerogels are influenced by the initial concentration of Ti3C2TX MXene dispersion. An increase in Ti3C2TX MXene concentration results in higher permittivity values, with M/G-12/0.5 and M/G-12/1 displaying greater ε' and ε" values compared to M/G-8/0.5 and M/G-8/1 (Figure S7, Supporting Information). This observation indicates that a higher concentration of MXene suspension leads to a denser 3D porous network, which, while enhancing electrical conductivity, may compromise impedance matching. Thus, excessive MXene concentration can result in increased permittivity values and reduced EMW absorption performance.

As previously discussed, the electromagnetic wave (EMW) attenuation behavior of MXene/GQDs aerogels primarily depends on dielectric loss. This dielectric loss is typically quantified using the dielectric loss tangent (tanδ e = ε″/ε') [47]. As illustrated in Fig. 4c, pure MXene aerogel exhibits higher tanδ e values compared to MXene/GQDs aerogels in the high-frequency range (>10 GHz), indicating that the pure MXene aerogel has a stronger dielectric loss capability. In contrast, the tanδ e values of MXene/GQDs aerogels with varying GQDs content show no significant differences. Attenuation constant (α) exhibits a comparable trend(Fig. 4d). Notably, the tanδ e curves for all samples display frequency-dependent resonant fluctuations, which are indicative of multiple dielectric relaxation behaviors. Usually, according to Debye relaxation theory, Cole-Cole semicircles mean the presence of relaxation behaviors, and each semicircle embodies one relaxation process in the curve of ε' versus ε″[48,49]. Fig. 4g and Figure S8 demonstrate that the ε'-ε″ plots for all samples exhibit several distinct semicircles, suggesting that multiple Debye relaxations contribute to dielectric loss. According to EMW dissipation mechanisms, dielectric loss arises from conductive and polarization losses [50]. Specifically, polarization loss comprises dipole and interfacial polarization. For absorbers with high electrical conductivity, conductive loss is a major mechanism for dissipating electromagnetic waves. The applied external electromagnetic field induces the formation of eddy currents in conductive materials, which convert electromagnetic energy into Joule heat [51,52]. As noted, the incorporation of GQDs increases the barrier to electron hopping and migration in MXene/GQDs aerogels, resulting in reduced conductive loss for aerogels with higher GQD content. Regarding dipolar polarization, structural defects and heteroatoms create asymmetric charge distributions that form electric dipoles [53,54]. These dipoles rotate but cannot keep pace with the rapidly changing external electromagnetic field, leading to polarization relaxation and subsequent EMW attenuation. The presence of structural defects and terminal groups (-O, -OH) on both Ti3C2TX MXene sheets and the GQD carbon skeleton significantly contributes to dipolar polarization and EMW attenuation. Additionally, according to the Maxwell-Wagner effect, charge accumulation at the heterogeneous interface between Ti3C2TX MXene and GQDs tends to trigger a capacitor-like microscopic dipole under an alternating electromagnetic field, thus resulting in interfacial polarization to enhance EMW dissipation. In this study, the addition of GQDs not only introduces more terminal groups and structural defects but also brings in heterogeneous interfaces in MXene/GQDs aerogels. This results in increased dipolar and interfacial polarization, thereby improving EMW attenuation from pure MXene to M/G-8/2. Overall, the dielectric loss behavior of MXene/GQDs aerogels arises from the cooperation of conductive loss and dipolar/interfacial polarization. Aerogels with higher GQD content exhibit stronger dipolar and interfacial polarization but lower conductive loss.

Noted that while the dielectric loss capability of pure MXene aerogel is stronger than that of MXene/GQDs aerogels in the high-frequency range, this finding is inconsistent with the EMW absorption behaviors depicted in the 2D RL contours. According to the microwave attenuation principles, EMW absorption performance depends on both the EMW attenuation capacity and impedance matching [55,56]. The impedance matching determines how well incident EMW penetrates the absorbers. Typically, impedance matching is assessed using the normalized impedance Z, which indicates the reflection capability of the absorbers with respect to incident EMW. An impedance value of Z = 1 signifies that the incident EMW is fully absorbed with no reflection. In the 2D plots of Z values, the area proportion where 0.8 ≤Z ≤ 1.2 is used to evaluate impedance matching degree [57–59]. A larger area indicates better impedance matching. Fig. 4h displays the impedance value maps for MXene/GQDs aerogels with varying GQDs content. Clearly, pure MXene aerogel demonstrates poor impedance matching due to its high electrical conductivity, which adversely affects EMW penetration due to the skin effect. The incorporation of GQDs reduces electrical conductivity and thus improves the impedance matching of MXene/GQDs aerogels. In addition, we can observe that the impedance matching of M/G-8/2, which contains a higher content of GQDs, is actually worse than that of M/G-8/1. In fact, the increase in the amount of GQDs has two opposing effects. On one hand, it reduces the conductivity of MXene, thereby improving the impedance mismatch issue caused by the excessive conductivity of MXene. On the other hand, it leads to a more developed network structure in the MXene/GQD aerogel, providing more conductive pathways for electron hopping and migration, thus worsening the impedance matching. In the case of M/G-8/1, the former factor dominates, while in M/G-8/2, the latter factor becomes dominant. Therefore, the impedance matching of M/G-8/2 is worse than that of M/G-8/1. Consequently, it is the suitable complex permittivity that accounts for optimal impedance matching and suitable dielectric loss to bring about superior EMW absorption performance for M/G-8/1.

The EMW attenuation mechanism of MXene/GQDs hybrid aerogels is thus illustrated in Fig. 5. Upon encountering an absorber, incident EMW undergoes reflection, transmission, and absorption. The reflected and transmitted portions of the EMW return to the air, while the absorbed EMW is dissipated through mechanisms including multiple scattering, polarization loss (dipole/interfacial polarization) and conductive loss [60,61]. In MXene/GQDs aerogels, the abundant terminal groups (-O, -OH) and structural defects present on both Ti3C2TX MXene sheets and the carbon skeleton of GQDs induce an asymmetric charge distribution for dipolar polarization. Additionally, the heterogeneous interface between MXene and GQDs generates charge accumulation, which results in interfacial polarization and enhances EMW dissipation. The 3D porous network, formed by MXene with high electrical conductivity, provides a continuous electron conduction path that facilitates conductive loss. Although the addition of GQDs may reduce conductive loss, it improves the impedance matching of MXene/GQDs aerogels. Furthermore, the 3D porous network introduces numerous interfaces and tortuous spaces that enhance multiple scattering and reflection, thereby confining the EMW within the absorber and promoting EMW consumption [62,63]. Consequently, the high EMW absorption performance of MXene/GQDs aerogels results from the combined effects of various dissipation mechanisms and optimized impedance matching.

In summary, MXene/GQDs hybrid aerogels have been successfully fabricated, with their 3D porous network microstructures controllably adjusted through the addition of GQDs. The introduction of GQDs, that contribute to generating abundant 0D/2D heterojunction in 3D MXene/GQDs hybrid aerogels, significantly enhances the EMW absorption performance by optimizing impedance matching and boosting dielectric loss. The 3D MXene/GQDs aerogels exhibit impressive EMW response characteristics (RLmin= −53.21, EAB = 7.90), outperforming most MXene-based aerogels. This work offers an innovative approach for developing high-efficiency MXene-based materials for EMW absorption.
